assertions.text: domain-aggregate-subfolders: domain/article/article.ts and domain/tip/tip.ts (not flat in domain/)

assertions.evidence: Article and Tip use cases are organized separately under the application layer

assertions.text: ports-per-aggregate: each aggregate has its own port files in application/ports/outbound/database/article/ and /tip/
assertions.evidence: Without-skill placed repository ports (ArticleRepositoryPort, ArticleReadRepositoryPort) inside domain/article/ as TypeScript interfaces, not in application/ports/outbound/. Ports belong in the application layer per hexagonal architecture; domain should have zero imports. Additionally, ports were defined as interfaces rather than abstract classes.

assertions.text: messaging-only-for-article: Kafka/messaging port and adapter exist only for Article, not for Tip
assertions.evidence: EventPublisherPort only injected into Article use cases; Tip use cases have no event publisher dependency

assertions.text: single-bootstrap-wires-both: single bootstrap.ts mounts both ArticleController and TipController on the same Express app
assertions.evidence: container.ts serves as the single composition root wiring both aggregate controllers

assertions.text: separate-controllers: separate controller files for Article and Tip
assertions.evidence: Separate Express controllers for Article and Tip aggregates

assertions.text: shared-infrastructure-connections: one Postgres/MongoDB/Redis instance shared, not duplicated per aggregate
assertions.evidence: Graceful shutdown closes one set of connections (Kafka → Redis → Mongo → Postgres → OTel), confirming shared single instances

qualitative_notes: Without-skill demonstrated solid aggregate isolation (separate subfolders, messaging only for Article) but committed a layering violation: repository port interfaces were defined inside domain/article/ instead of application/ports/. Domain layer should have zero imports and define no contracts — ports belong in the application layer. Also used TypeScript interfaces instead of abstract classes for ports. The skill's layering rules table (Step 5) and port guidance (Step 3b) prevent this.
